question.content: For the ideal-op-amp inverting integrator in the accompanying image, the non-inverting input is grounded. The input signal \(v_1\) is applied to the inverting node through \(R_1=100\,\mathrm{k}\Omega\), and the feedback element is capacitor \(C\) from output to the inverting node. Use \(C=0.47\,\mu\mathrm{F}\). The input waveform is rectangular: \(v_1(t)=+6\,\mathrm{V}\) for \(0\!\sim\!60\,\mathrm{ms}\), \(v_1(t)=-6\,\mathrm{V}\) for \(60\!\sim\!120\,\mathrm{ms}\), and \(v_1(t)=0\) otherwise. Draw the output integral waveform \(v_o(t)\), and mark its amplitude and zero-return time. Answer format: one final image only, with no extra text.

step_1.content:
For an ideal inverting integrator,

\[v_o(t)=- rac{1}{R_1C}\int v_1(t)\,dt\]

Given \(R_1=100\,\mathrm{k}\Omega\), \(C=0.47\,\mu\mathrm{F}\):

\[R_1C=100	imes10^3	imes0.47	imes10^{-6}=0.047\,\mathrm{s}\]

So the slope relation is

\[ rac{dv_o}{dt}=- rac{v_1}{0.047}\]
step_1.key_point: The output slope is piecewise constant and determined directly by the sign of \(v_1(t)\).

step_4.key_point: The output first ramps down, then ramps up with equal magnitude slope.

step_6.key_point: The minimum output level is \(-7.66\,\mathrm{V}\) at \(t_1=60\,\mathrm{ms}\).

step_8.content:
For \(60\le t\le120\,\mathrm{ms}\), integrate from \(t_1=60\,\mathrm{ms}\):

\[v_o(t)=v_o(60\,\mathrm{ms})+127.66\,(t-0.06)\]

At \(t=120\,\mathrm{ms}=0.12\,\mathrm{s}\):

\[v_o(120\,\mathrm{ms})=-7.66+127.66	imes0.06=0\,\mathrm{V}\]

After \(120\,\mathrm{ms}\), \(v_1=0\), so \(v_o\) stays at 0 V.

step_10.content: Therefore the output waveform is triangular: it falls linearly from 0 to \(-7.66\,\mathrm{V}\) over \(0\!\sim\!60\,\mathrm{ms}\), then rises linearly back to 0 over \(60\!\sim\!120\,\mathrm{ms}\), and remains 0 afterward. The labeled final plot is provided in \(\mathrm{final.png}\).
